# Transcrição: 08-Scaling Example Using V-Scale, H-Scale & M-Scale 

- **Fonte:** 08-Scaling Example Using V-Scale, H-Scale & M-Scale 
- **Duração:** 12:50
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:05]

In this example of some scaling strategies that we put in place some months ago on this particular ad account, I want to demonstrate how we actually tested multiple different ways of scaling, what worked and what didn't. And what was quite interesting at this time was we were really keen to see whether auto bid or manual bid would kind of perform better. And also the impact of horizontal scaling versus vertical scaling. So in this particular ad account, although it's not running right now, it was the best example of using multiple strategies to see which ones would give us performance and scale as well. So over this period, we spent $35k. The target for this particular ad account, so it's a subscription e-commerce, was to get our cost per purchase below $80 and our cost per account creation at about the $30 mark. So you can see we're kind of just there or thereabouts. But this particular period was all about testing. So I've just selected my scale campaigns to show you the top of funnel of what was going on. In this account, we had warm and hot and kind of re-targeting would bring in the overall cost per purchase lower, etc, etc. But for this particular example, I want to show you kind of what we went through and how we set this up as well. Now, first of all, in our grad testing phase, we found for this particular ad account that look-alikes and interests were okay. But when we went broad, we actually got much better performance. And that came through a lot of kind of priming and kind of understanding the avatar and creating ads. And what we really focused on on this ad account was creating ads that clearly either opted people into the ad or opted them out. So if they saw it, and they knew it wasn't for them, they scroll past. But if the ad resonated, then they would click. And that works extremely well to make this broad campaign work for us. So we started off with a campaign which is this one here, $123 a day. It started off at $100. We put some automations in place just to bump up the budget. And hence, it's a kind of decimal value there. And then from that point, we kind of just left it. If it performed well, budgets increased if it didn't, and it didn't increase. But that just continued ticking over in the background. You can see the results were okay. But we wanted to beat that with higher bids as well and see how far we could push the budgets. And as you may have seen, if you try to scale up, sometimes, if not often, when you try and scale up, the performance doesn't maintain. So from that point forward, we said, right, let's let's try a few different strategies. The first one was actually testing DCO dynamic content, optimize ads out. And seeing if,

## [03:05 - 06:09]

you know, we knew that our audiences worked. So for example, we had broad split by age groups. So this is a campaign that was targeting women already. So then what we wanted to do was to see if we split the age groups and created dynamic content optimized ads specifically for those age groups. So that's literally just creating one ad with our top creatives in place for that age group based on our previous testing. And actually, that that was quite interesting. We found pockets of audiences where our cost per full account creation was much lower than our $30 target. Actually, that performed really well. So following that, the question was, right, we could scale up the spend, we could duplicate by doing a horizontal scaling. What what what could we do to kind of make that work? At this stage, we introduced a new campaign, which was running at the time, it was just the called the great campaign. And we ran that again, into our broad audience to test. And at the same time, we then push that into horizontal scaling. So horizontal scaling, if you've been through the training is literally taking your winning campaign, and copying it. And that's all we did. So we copied it out. This one at the time was $500. It's increased over the time. This one, we just copied it straight into an $1800 account. And it was coming to towards the end of the campaign period. But we wanted to see what the performance would be like against the original. And as you can see at the top level, if I just highlight these two campaigns here, the duplicated campaign did not perform as well as the original. And this goes back to my point and something which I noticed more and more, you know, especially now and kind of since 2018 onwards, that simple duplication at the higher budget as well as it used to work pre 2018 doesn't work as well after 2018. So that that was part of the test there. Then the next part of it was, let's try and run some bid caps and see what kind of performance we can get from bid cap, testing that versus target cost, and testing that against cost caps. So these were the three manual bidding strategies we deployed to see what kind of results we could get. Now, target cost actually gave us overall the best performance. So the cost per full account creation was $36, still higher than our target. But of all the three bidding options, it gave us the best performance. However, with target cost, it would not spend. You know, it's the problem, the thing with target cost is it will try and achieve your target, but it may not spend as much. We then did cost cap, which was based on the amount that we've associated

## [06:09 - 09:14]

with our value for what we're producing. Now, we're optimizing for the purchase event, even though it's not our main goal, as we found that overall, for this account, it still gave us strong metrics for the top of funnel metric we're aiming for, and converting well at the bottom of the funnel as well. So our cost per acquisition target, we're aiming to push this down to $50 for a purchase. And again, going back to the example of how manual bid works, when we set our cost cap at the $62.50 mark, we actually got better delivery and better results. So again, with cost cap, although the intent is tell Facebook the CPA you want, and you know, the lame to get it, we found that actually bidding higher gave us a better performance than bidding at the actual amount, and bidding lower, we just didn't get the delivery. So that's just kind of the breakdown of cost cap. Then when you look at target cost, and I expand that down, that doesn't want to expand down. So let's click into it. So in looking at target cost, again, we've gone with the target cost amount, and a target that's lower and higher, and you can see how that came out in the results. So in this case, although we got a better cost per account creation here, we didn't quite get the delivery on this particular campaign. And I think it ran maybe for a day or so just to see what kind of data came out. And you know, interestingly, the most of the spend actually went into the target cost, which was our kind of one times our CPA bid. And actually, as we start to bid higher, then our cost went up as well. So it was an interesting test just to see how the auction would react to target cost. And then if I look at our bid cost, so if I find a highlight that, so we look at bid cost here, this one, I base it on kind of multiples of what our CPA target is. So if our CPA target here is $50, we'd go up to twice, three times and four times that level. And generally, the best bid does fall out between kind of two and three times your CPA target. So and that's kind of what we found. So $50, we didn't get the spend coming through. But we did start to see spend and performance at the higher levels. But with all of our manual bids, and we actually ran a split test for this as well, we were not able to get as strong a performance as we did with our non manual bid campaigns, because we were letting Facebook choose when and how to run their manual bids. And therefore, we pushed the highest spend into those ad account that's to the campaigns that were running on auto bid. So this is just to illustrate an example of some of the thought process that goes into this. Some of the testing, you know, one thing you could critique about this is we could have gone on for

## [09:14 - 12:16]

testing the bid levels for longer. However, the spend levels were still fairly significant to give us good insight as to kind of what was happening. And you know, bear in mind, when you're doing this kind of live testing, you don't want to have a negative impact on your winners. So for example, this one, the top campaign was consistently performing fairly well. The more you test with bid caps and duplications and stuff like that, you can really unbalance the account. So we wanted to just really balance that out in terms of performance to make sure that our main campaign that's bringing in the good performance is not destabilized with what's going on, and that we can still continue testing alongside it as well. It was a broad audience. So our audience sizes are that much bigger. So we have less of a concern about audience overlap and things like that. But the longer you do have audience overlap, the bigger the impact could be. So if we ran our manual bids for too long for, you know, five, 10 days, whilst running our main campaign, you would end up with fluctuating results as we've discussed previously around auction overlap. So, you know, this is what one of the things I want you to take away from this is we spent a lot of time, you know, implementing the BPM method, but optimizing every part as much as we could. So that enabled broad campaigns to work really well for us. It enabled us to make DCO works at the work at the top of the funnel. So dynamic content optimized ads are great for running, you know, multiple variants. And I generally run this lower down in the funnel as a set and forget. So once I find a winning angle for my warm and hot audiences, it does literally become a set and forget. However, it can work top of funnel as well, as long as you've optimized other aspects of your funnel. Now, now consider this, we're running broad campaigns with dynamic content optimized ads with no bids. That is at almost as hands off, as you can imagine, for running an account at decent scale. So this is one month spend, for example, 35k, pretty much not needing so much of our maintenance and control, aside from the fact that we're doing lots of testing in the meantime, these campaigns didn't require much optimization in terms of kind of just keeping them going. So it's just kind of bearing in mind the balance between wanting to really, really squeeze up performance and push up your spend versus, you know, for example, this one ended up with $1,500 a day spend, we edged it up through v scaling. So it was moderate, it was stable, and it still enabled us to scale up to that $1,500 a day mark at the campaign level. But without the stress of worrying about manual bid and firefighting different performance issues and things like that. And this is how I like to

## [12:16 - 12:50]

operate my ad accounts to balance between risk and reward. In some cases, you do want to scale up faster and you need to introduce therefore a bit more risk. But in many cases, when you're running your ad account to help your business or a client's business to grow, you can do it in a more structured and stable way, as long as you've put the effort in ahead of that, to develop everything that we've talked about in the BPM method, so that the scaling strategy that you apply is the one that balances your particular risk and reward in the best way possible.
